indexSubtitle: Drag, drop, share. Physics that fits in a tweet.
indexBetaNotice: Playgrounds are in active development. Expect rough edges — feedback welcome.

controls.removeHint: Long-press / right-click a body to remove

orbital-mechanics.title: Orbital Mechanics
orbital-mechanics.subtitle: N-body gravitational chaos
orbital-mechanics.indexCardDescription: Drop bodies, drag to set velocity, watch the chaos unfold.
orbital-mechanics.aiPrompt: I'm exploring an n-body gravitational simulation. Walk me through what's happening physically — the forces involved, why orbits form (or don't), and what makes this configuration stable or chaotic. Use the current state as your starting point.

info.title: What you're seeing
info.intro: Each dot is a point mass attracted to every other by gravity (Newton's 1/r²). The integrator is symplectic Velocity-Verlet, so energy is conserved over long runs.
info.chaos: With three or more bodies the motion becomes chaotic — there is no closed-form solution. Tiny changes in starting conditions blow up exponentially.
interactions: Click empty space to drop a new body
Drag a body to fling it (drag length = velocity)
Long-press / right-click a body to remove it
Pinch or scroll to zoom, two-finger drag to pan

presetsInfo.figure-8: Three equal masses chase each other along a single ∞-shaped track. A choreographic solution discovered by Chenciner and Montgomery (2000). Zero total momentum, zero angular momentum.
presetsInfo.solar-mini: A Sun-like body with three small orbiting planets. Stable Keplerian orbits — each planet sweeps equal areas in equal times.
presetsInfo.pythagorean: Burrau's 1913 problem: masses 3, 4, 5 placed at rest on a 3-4-5 right triangle. Notoriously chaotic — bodies eject and rejoin unpredictably.
presetsInfo.random-cluster: Five random masses with random velocities. Every reload is the same seed, but every preset switch in your session looks different physical.
